data.title: Fiverr: Likely Value Trap In The Age Of AI
data.description:
The last time I reviewed Fiverr International Ltd. (FVRR) was in late 2024. At the time I liked that this founder-led company seemed to be leaning into AI, and I thought it might be a beneficiary of a growing gig economy, which is why I gave the company a “Buy” rating.

That’s probably been one (if not my worst) call, as the stock is down more than 60% since the article was published. In 2026 alone, FVRR is down over 50%, while the S&P 500 is down just 7%.

With the increasing capabilities of artificial intelligence, especially the new features of Claude (like Cowork and Code), there’s a huge expectation these tools are going to wipe out a lot of freelance work.

Whether that happens, only time will tell. Let’s dive into the company's plan to combat AI and discuss the company’s business metrics as well as recent financial results.

Will Fiverr's 4 Pillar Plan Be Enough to Combat AI?

In the back half of 2025, Fiverr’s management team announced a multi-year company-wide restructuring plan focused on four key pillars. On the company’s recent earnings call, co-founder and CEO Micha Kaufman outlined this four-pillar plan:

A few months ago, we initiated a company-wide restructuring to accelerate this shift. We have since developed a multiyear execution plan built around 4 pillars: the first is matching, building advanced semantic and reasoning layer powered by proprietary data to enable AI-native talent matching. The second is product, transforming the experience across matching, fulfillment, collaboration and talent management. The third is go-to-market, expanding into enterprise and AI-native distribution channels with scalable growth engines. The fourth is operational excellence, becoming an AI-native organization across engineering, product and operations.

Kaufman went on to say he’s expecting tangible results from this plan in 4 to 6 quarters and believes it will lead to continued revenue expansion.

I’d say the company essentially had to make a strategic move like this one because the business has to move upmarket because AI is eating the small, simple tasks. It’s interesting the company actually noted this risk in their prior year 20-F filing, stating:

AI may present challenges for our industry. There is a risk that the evolving AI landscape could reduce the demand for some of our simple service offerings within the writing, translation, and graphic and design verticals.

In hindsight, “may present challenges” is a massive understatement, as I’d say it’s the primary challenge for the business now. The business is hoping Fiverr Pro can continue to drive upmarket adoption through more premium offerings like the following:

There are some positives for Fiverr, as numerous metrics illustrate the company still has a solid take rate and is expanding wallet share:

It’ll be interesting to see if these numbers continue to hold up as tools like Claude and Gemini continue to improve. Fiverr certainly must continue to expand their AI-native offerings. The company did recently launch an AI video hub. On the whole, it seems like a good move. Fiverr must continue thinking outside the box and create similar offerings going forward.

Fiverr still sees a large market opportunity, which they noted in their last Investor Presentation:

I have to say this TAM assumption seems very bold to me given the situation Fiverr is facing. The core issue is that a good portion of the traditional freelance market, like writing, graphic design, translation, and entry-level coding, is precisely what AI tools are disrupting. If AI continues to commoditize these lower-end services, the effective addressable market for a platform like Fiverr could be materially smaller than the figure above suggests.

It does seem like the gig economy is growing, and many think this trend will continue. I’d venture to guess it does as AI layoffs in industries like technology increase. Yet, an increase in freelancers doesn't automatically translate into growth on Fiverr's platform, especially if buyers turn to AI tools directly.

Let’s next discuss business challenges, as there are more risks than simply just AI.

Risks

Fiverr lists numerous items that might impact the business going forward. I’m going to touch on a few risks noted in the company’s Risk Factors section in their most recent 20-F filing.

Competition remains a top risk. I’d put that into two buckets. You have competition from AI tools, and you have competition from other companies employing freelancers, like Upwork (UPWK). While Fiverr is a known name in the space, compared to Upwork, it’s historically focused on what I’d say are “simple” projects, thus management’s big push to get the business upmarket.

Over the past few years, the business has been able to focus more on high-quality buyers as total buyers have gone down, yet annual spend per buyer is improving, as you can see below:

Fiverr’s challenge will be keeping buyers using the platform. Fiverr noted in the company’s shareholder letter they don’t think these key performance indicators are vital given the new plan. However, I’d say these metrics are important because Fiverr can’t keep relying on higher-value customers. I’d say they need to try to add users or at least stop the bleeding, yet I’m not sure if that’s possible given the AI alternatives.

Fiverr does have cash on hand as their balance sheet remains in solid shape (which I’ll discuss more below). Management has stated capital will likely go to M&A. This could certainly enhance Fiverr’s offerings and perhaps even help the business acquire new buyers. Yet it could lead to Peter Lynch’s infamous “diworsification.” Given the current state of affairs, any unsuccessful M&A activity would certainly hurt the company's financial position (for example, wasting cash or having goodwill impairment).

Finally, from a risk perspective, I’d say Fiverr could be impacted by worsening macro conditions. While layoffs could lead to more gig workers, as many of Fiverr’s customers are SMBs, worsening conditions like inflation or a recession would certainly affect Fiverr from both a seller and buyer perspective.

Financials

Before diving into the current 20-F filing, let’s zoom out and see how well Fiverr has performed on an annual basis over the last few years. As you can see from the below graphic from the company’s latest Investor Presentation, the company has seen revenue growth continue over the last few years:

Furthermore, despite increases in investments (for example, the company’s AI capabilities), Fiverr’s net income and earnings per share have continuously improved, as you can see below:

Additionally, thanks to share buybacks, the company’s share count has also declined, which is a positive for investors. Management noted that as of December 31, 2025, the company still has $67.5 million left on the current authorization for the buyback program. Although, I’d like to see the company use their cash to focus on internal investments or M&A.

These trends are positive, but things change when you look at upcoming guidance. For fiscal year 2026, management is expecting revenue in the range of $380 million to $420 million, which would be a decline in the range of 3% to 12%. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be in the range of $60 to $80 million. That’s quite a drop from the $92 million that company earned in fiscal year 2025.

Fiverr still has a solid balance sheet, as you can see below, with enough current assets to cover all of their current liabilities and no long-term debt:

Valuation

Fiverr has a valuation grade of an “A+” at Seeking Alpha:

Whichever way you choose to value this company, all these metrics would lead an investor to think Fiverr is trading at a very cheap valuation. As it’s a profitable, more established company, I’d say price to earnings is the best valuation metric for this business.

If you look at the company's forward P/E ratio (Non-GAAP), it's lower than the sector median and significantly lower than the company's historic five-year averages. In the below graphic, you can see Fiverr's current forward P/E ratio is at an all-time low:

From a different viewpoint, I have updated my reverse discounted cash flow model using the latest cash flow figures for Fiverr. Using a discount rate of 10% and a terminal rate of 2%, if you look at analysts' revenue growth estimates, most believe Fiverr's revenue will be flat or grow in the low single digits on average for the next several years. If Fiverr can grow even by a meager 1%, it would be viewed as undervalued, as you can see below:

Conclusion

Historically, Fiverr has proven over the last few years that despite losing buyers, they have been able to get their current buyers to spend more and thus have continued to generate revenue growth.

2026 is shaping up to be a much different story. On one hand, I think there has been an exaggeration as to how quickly AI implementation will impact businesses, especially SMBs. Yet, Claude’s new capabilities are impressive, and given the fact Google and Microsoft are spending immensely on capex, AI tools and enhancements will only make life easier for many buyers who would have traditionally used freelancers.

As Kaufman noted on the earnings call, “There's a prevailing narrative that AI eliminates labor. That framing is incomplete.”

While incomplete, I’d say it will eliminate at least some labor, and despite a growing gig economy, I’m not sure if Fiverr will be able to compete with such intense competition.

While the stock is probably oversold at the minute, I’d only look at this as a trading, bounce-back play. The long-term narrative is too uncertain despite the cheap valuation. I'll be watching and waiting to see how Kaufman’s new strategy plays out in 2026.
